paraphrase: The Starks ruled the north for thousands of years and are credited with defending it from raiders, slavers, Andals, wargs, and wildlings; a Stark is said to have built the Wall.
Barbrey Dustin herself says the northmen love the Starks.
Ned Stark in particular was renowned for his honour and sense of justice, winning great respect and loyalty across the north.
The Boltons are infamous for flaying their enemies and wearing their skins as cloaks.
Roose Bolton is portrayed as cruel, cold, and creepy, known for his habit of leeching himself.
Ramsay is called a murderous 'beast in human skin' who hunts and rapes women for sport.
Ramsay betrays and kills northmen at the sack of Winterfell, and separately kills the Lady of Hornwood.
Roose Bolton sends northern armies on what amounts to a suicide mission at Duskendale.
At the Red Wedding, the Boltons betray King Robb, participate in the breaking of guest right, and help slaughter hundreds of northmen.
The video invokes the refrain that every northern lord at Winterfell lost family at the Red Wedding, and that 'the north remembers.'
verbatim: “Our foes are not as formidable as they appear,” Ser Justin assured Asha on the first day of the march. “Roose Bolton is feared, but little loved. And his friends the Freys … the north has not forgotten the Red Wedding. Every lord at Winterfell lost kinsmen there. Stannis need only bloody Bolton, and the northmen will abandon him.”

paraphrase: Wyman Manderly secretly meets with Davos Seaworth in Dance while outwardly pretending loyalty to the Boltons and Freys.
Wyman gives a speech about 'the debt' the Manderlys owe to House Stark.
Wyman speaks of the 'murder' of his son Wendel at the Red Wedding.

spoken_quote: The north remembers, and the mummer's farce is almost done
paraphrase: Wyman tells Davos, 'The north remembers, and the mummer's farce is almost done.'
verbatim: “Soon I must return to the feast to toast my friends of Frey,” Manderly continued. “They watch me, ser. Day and night their eyes are on me, noses sniffing for some whiff of treachery. You saw them, the arrogant Ser Jared and his nephew Rhaegar, that smirking worm who wears a dragon’s name. Behind them both stands Symond, clinking coins. That one has bought and paid for several of my servants and two of my knights. One of his wife’s handmaids has found her way into the bed of my own fool. If Stannis wonders that my letters say so little, it is because I dare not even trust my maester. Theomore is all head and no heart. You heard him in my hall. Maesters are supposed to put aside old loyalties when they don their chains, but I cannot forget that Theomore was born a Lannister of Lannisport and claims some distant kinship to the Lannisters of Casterly Rock. Foes and false friends are all around me, Lord Davos. They infest my city like roaches, and at night I feel them crawling over me.” The fat man’s fingers coiled into a fist, and all his chins trembled. “My son Wendel came to the Twins a guest. He ate Lord Walder’s bread and salt, and hung his sword upon the wall to feast with friends. And they murdered him. Murdered, I say, and may the Freys choke upon their fables. I drink with Jared, jape with Symond, promise Rhaegar the hand of my own beloved granddaughter … but never think that means I have forgotten. The north remembers, Lord Davos. The north remembers, and the mummer’s farce is almost done. My son is home.”

paraphrase: With the help of an ironborn boy named Wex, Wyman has learned the truth of the sack of Winterfell: it was Ramsay Bolton, not Theon Greyjoy, who burned the castle and killed its people.
Wyman reveals Bran and Rickon were not killed by Theon and are still alive, with Rickon believed to be hiding on the island of Skagos.
Wyman sends Davos to Skagos to fetch Rickon so the north can rally around a living Stark against the Boltons.
If Davos succeeds in bringing back Rickon, Wyman says he will accept Stannis as King, with Rickon installed as Lord of Winterfell.
Robett Glover is present at the meeting with Davos and may be in contact with his brother Galbart; there are hints Robett may raise an army.
Wyman says Houses Locke and Flint of Widow's Watch follow his lead, and a member of House Locke is seen speaking up against Ramsay.

House Umber may also be involved: the Umbers are split, with 'Crowfood' Umber siding with Stannis while 'Whoresbane' Umber only sides with the Boltons because the Freys hold his nephew the Greatjon hostage.
Roose Bolton himself calls the Umbers 'cunning' and says he fears them.
The Umbers were ordered back in Clash to build a fleet of warships with the Manderlys, and by Dance that fleet is complete and 'hidden' up the White Knife river.

In the besieged Winterfell chapters, someone builds snowmen shaped like four specific northern lords: Wyman Manderly, Whoresbane Umber, Harwood Stout, and Barbrey Dustin -- read as marking out a conspiratorial group.
Harwood Stout, Barbrey Dustin's sworn man, is seen talking quietly with Whoresbane Umber.
Immediately after that conversation, Barbrey Dustin becomes strangely interested in the Winterfell crypts and has Theon take her down there.
In the crypts, Barbrey notices that three of the statues of dead Starks are missing their swords.

The three missing swords are the ones taken by Osha, Meera Reed, and Bran when they escaped Winterfell, which corroborates Wex's story that Bran and Rickon are alive and that the Boltons lied about the sack of Winterfell.
Barbrey's sudden interest in the crypts suggests she heard Wex's story from Wyman, possibly relayed through the Harwood-Whoresbane conversation.
Wyman is established at White Harbor to use long visits to the privy as cover for secret meetings, and it's hinted he continues this habit at Winterfell.
After visiting the crypts, Barbrey speaks boldly to the Freys about the people killed at the Red Wedding and says, 'The north remembers.'
In Clash, Robb Stark says that if he dies, he wants Jon Snow to succeed him as King in the North.
At that point Robb believes Arya, Bran, and Rickon are all dead, and Sansa is married to Tyrion Lannister -- the reasoning behind turning to Jon.
Robb decides the best course is to legitimize Jon as a Stark.
Robb meets with several of his lords and has them sign a document declaring Jon his heir.
The witnesses who sign Robb's will include Catelyn Stark, Edmure Tully, Greatjon Umber, Jason Mallister, Maege Mormont, and Galbart Glover.
The Greatjon and Jason Mallister are held prisoner (by the Freys/Lannisters), so they 'probably can't do much' to act on the will.
Maege Mormont and Galbart Glover are sent on a mission to Howland Reed in the Neck, and evidence (the crannogmen following through on Robb's Moat Cailin orders) suggests they actually arrive there.

Howland Reed is the only living survivor of the fight at the Tower of Joy, where it was discovered that Jon is the son of Lyanna Stark and Rhaegar Targaryen, potentially giving Jon a claim on the Iron Throne.

Catelyn Stark, another witness to the will, is resurrected by Beric Dondarrion as Lady Stoneheart, who leads the Brotherhood without Banners on a murderous campaign against anyone connected to the Red Wedding.
During the siege of Riverrun, the singer Tom of Sevenstreams spies on the Lannister-Frey camp for the Brotherhood, tipping Stoneheart off about traveling Freys so she can capture and kill them.
Fans speculate Tom may also learn of Lannister prisoner transports and an upcoming Lannister wedding, feeding a theorized Brotherhood/riverlords plot to free prisoners and stage a 'Red Wedding 2.0.'
Stoneheart is shown traveling in or near Hag's Mire -- where Robb's will was signed -- before heading up into the Neck, the same place Howland, Maege, and Galbart are.
Stoneheart carries Robb's old crown, meaning she holds the literal power to crown the next King in the North.
In life Catelyn disliked Jon as a living reminder of Ned's infidelity, which could make Stoneheart reluctant to crown him.
Howland Reed is the one person who could tell Catelyn/Stoneheart that Jon is not really Ned's bastard but Lyanna's son by Rhaegar, potentially removing that obstacle.
Lem says Stoneheart cannot speak because her throat was cut too deep, but adds that 'she remembers.'
verbatim: “She don’t speak,” said the big man in the yellow cloak. “You bloody bastards cut her throat too deep for that. But she remembers.” He turned to the dead woman and said, “What do you say, m’lady? Was he part of it?”

paraphrase: Edmure Tully, a Frey prisoner at Riverrun and another will-witness, is at one point left alone with Tom o' Sevens, the Brotherhood's spy, raising the possibility he passes on information.
Jaime later lets Edmure re-enter Riverrun to end the siege, giving him the chance to conspire with Tully men, including his uncle Brynden 'the Blackfish' Tully.
Brynden Blackfish is loyal to House Stark, and Jaime himself says he doesn't doubt Brynden will keep fighting.
Unlike the show (where Brynden dies at Riverrun), in the books Edmure helps Brynden escape, leaving him free to fight for House Stark and possibly aware of the will/succession plot.
Desmond Grell and Robin Ryger, the Tully master-at-arms and captain of the guard, leave Riverrun and choose to join the Night's Watch at the Wall, where Jon is stationed.
Fans speculate these two trusted Tully men were sent by Edmure specifically to contact Jon about the plot to crown him.
The video explicitly notes there is 'no direct evidence' that any of these Riverrun/Neck figures are actually plotting over Robb's will -- the connections are suspicious but unproven.
Part of Stannis's army marching on Winterfell is made up of northmen -- mountain clansmen, some Mormonts, Glovers, and others -- who resent Stannis as a foreign stranger who worships a foreign god.
These northmen's real motive for marching is to kill Boltons and rescue 'Arya Stark' (actually Jeyne Poole impersonating her); the real Arya is in Braavos, unknown to the north.
Two leaders of the mountain clansmen, the Norrey and the Flint, come to visit Jon at the Wall, and Jon himself thinks they must have a special reason for the visit.
Fans suggest the clansmen know about Robb's will and come to the Wall to judge whether Jon could be a worthy King in the North.
Jon discusses his plans for the wildlings with the clansmen and tells them that though he may seem a green boy, he is still a son of Eddard Stark -- possibly exactly what they want to hear.
One possible link between the clansmen and the Neck conspiracy: they could be in touch with Maege Mormont through her daughter Alysane, who is on the march with Stannis.

Alternatively, the clansmen might connect through Galbart Glover's sister-in-law Sybelle Glover, who spends a suspiciously long time praying in the godswood -- echoing how Sansa received secret messages in the godswood back in Clash.
The clansmen are shown to know Bran Stark is alive -- they see him traveling north toward the Wall, and a clansman from House Liddle even meets him directly.
Bran is Robb's oldest living brother and would be his heir if not for the will, which skips him only because Robb believed him dead.
Notably, the clansmen do not tell Stannis that Bran is alive, showing they are keeping secrets of their own regardless of what side they're formally on.
Overall there is no clear evidence of an actual clansmen conspiracy, only that the northmen in Stannis's army are clearly more loyal to House Stark than to Stannis himself.

House Karstark: Arnolf Karstark nominally joins Stannis but secretly plots to betray him to the Boltons, partly by forcibly marrying Karhold heiress Alys Karstark to his son Cregan to seize the lordship himself -- a real northern conspiracy, but a pro-Bolton one, not part of the pro-Stark plotting.

spoken_quote: the last son of Eddard Stark
paraphrase: Alys Karstark escapes Arnolf's plot and turns to Jon Snow for protection, calling him 'the last son of Eddard Stark.'
verbatim: “Good. I was afraid … I begged my father to leave one of my brothers as castellan, but none of them wished to miss the glory and ransoms to be won in the south. Now Torr and Edd are dead. Harry was a prisoner at Maidenpool when last we heard, but that was almost a year ago. He may be dead as well. I did not know where else to turn but to the last son of Eddard Stark.”

paraphrase: There's no evidence Alys knows of any conspiracy, but her appeal to Jon reflects a broader northern perception of him as the true inheritor of Ned Stark's legacy.

In a published sample chapter from The Winds of Winter, Stannis discovers Arnolf's betrayal and locks up his men, taking the Karstarks out of action -- shown as an example of a conspiracy that did NOT stay secret.

House Hornwood: after Lord Halys and his heir Daryn die, Donella Hornwood becomes Lady, but Ramsay forcibly marries her and then starves her to death in a dungeon until she chews off her own fingers; Ramsay now styles himself Lord of Hornwood, and some Hornwood men have submitted to the Boltons.
Barbrey Dustin points out that the Hornwoods haven't forgotten what Ramsay did to Donella, implying latent disloyalty even without direct conspiracy evidence.
Houses Cerwyn and Tallhart: Roose sends their men on the suicide mission to Duskendale, and at the sack of Winterfell Ramsay betrays and kills Lord Cley Cerwyn and Leobald Tallhart.
Survivors of the fighting at Winterfell join Stannis's army and could spread the truth of Ramsay's crimes and betrayal there, which most of the north still blames on Theon Greyjoy.
Roose Bolton himself describes the Cerwyns and Tallharts as 'not to be relied on.'

House Ryswell appears to be the one major northern house genuinely loyal to House Bolton -- tied to them by marriage and working closely with Boltons and Freys; though they lost men at the Red Wedding too, there's no hint of Ryswell disloyalty.
Roose Bolton himself describes northern politics in Dance as a 'world of treachery and deceit.'
verbatim: “He should be. Fear is what keeps a man alive in this world of treachery and deceit. Even here in Barrowton the crows are circling, waiting to feast upon our flesh. The Cerwyns and the Tallharts are not to be relied on, my fat friend Lord Wyman plots betrayal, and Whoresbane … the Umbers may seem simple, but they are not without a certain low cunning. Ramsay should fear them all, as I do. The next time you see him, tell him that.”

paraphrase: The video's verdict: probably not one single unified 'Grand' conspiracy, but several separate, medium-sized, loosely overlapping plots.

Reason one: Westeros lacks fast, private communication -- ravens can be intercepted or lost, and messages are generally handled by maesters, who aren't fully trusted either (both Barbrey and Wyman are shown questioning maesters).

Reason two: the bigger a conspiracy is, the less likely it is to stay secret -- illustrated by the Karstark plot, where only a few men knew of Arnolf's scheme yet the truth still got out via Alys; Stannis himself makes the point that you can't keep secrets among lots and lots of people.

It looks instead like several separate plans: Manderly wants Rickon installed as Lord of Winterfell under King Stannis, while Maege and Galbart seemingly want Jon Snow crowned; Stoneheart and the mountain clansmen appear to have their own separate priorities rather than acting as one coordinated conspiracy.
